title: Seller Debt in Acquisitions of Private Firms: A Security Design Approach
abstract: We propose a security design model in which a potential acquirer approaches a firm with a value-add plan. The target has a single owner, who possesses private information: he alone knows whether his firm is compatible with the plan. The owner agrees that the acquirer will add value but believes that the value-add will not be as much as what the acquirer expects. Although the acquirer can choose any monotone limited liability security to offer along with cash, we show that, under general conditions, any security that is employed always takes the form of nonrecourse debt provided by the seller.

author.Ludovic Phalippou: Saïd Business School, University of Oxford,UK
author.Thomas Noe: Saïd Business School, University of Oxford,UK

article.1 Closely Related Literature:
Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

The aggregate acquisition value of private firms and public firm subsidiaries reached $9 trillion in the last two decades (Capital IQ). In most cases, sellers of these firms, unlike public firm shareholders, are compensated by a cash-equivalent consideration, plus perhaps one of three types of performance-sensitive security:1(1) seller debt, which is a nonrecourse debt claim issued by the seller and collateralized by the target’s assets (Jansen 2020); (2) seller equity, which refers to the seller retaining equity in thetargetasset (aka, rollover equity); and (3) earnouts, which are contingent payments based on the target’s performance. In dollar terms, seller debt is six times larger than earnouts and 12 times larger than seller equity (Everett 2020).

We develop a model of acquisition offer design. A prospective acquirer attempts to buy an asset for which she has a value-add plan, and her offer is a combination of cash and asecurity. The security can be any claim on the cash flows generated by the acquired asset, as long as it satisfies the standard limited liability and monotonicity conditions assumptions of the security design literature (e.g.,DeMarzo 2005). The seller has private information about whether or not the value-add plan is compatible with his asset. In the compatible case, the acquirer is more confident about the success of the value-add plan than the seller is. In the incompatible case, it is common knowledge that the value-add plan will not create any value.

Belief heterogeneity about the value-add plan pushes the offer towards more cash, while the desire to separate seller types pushes towards adding a security. In the absence of private information, the acquirer, being more confident, prefers to pay in cash to capture the value-add. However, because of adverse selection, her choice of cash does not allow her to distinguish between compatible and incompatible cases. To differentiate between the two, she needs to include seller securities that are sensitive to the seller’s private information.

Our paper examines which security the acquirer will offer. Because the seller’s return from more performance-sensitive securities varies more with the seller’s private information, the amount of security consideration required to screen out incompatible sellers decreases with the performance sensitivity of the security. Thus, more performance-sensitive securities (e.g., seller equity) allow for a larger fraction of cash in the offer. This “cash augmentation effect” favors more performance-sensitive securities. However, increasing the security performance-sensitivity also gives rise to a “confidence effect.” Because the acquirer is more confident than the seller about her value-add plan, she assigns a higher value than the seller does to any security claim on the acquired asset. More performance-sensitive securities augment this difference, and thus make offering more performance-sensitive securities costlier for acquirers.

The optimal security design is determined by the resolution of these two opposing forces: acquirer confidence pushing toward seller debt and adverse selection pushing toward more performance-sensitive securities like seller equity. We show that, under a mild distributional restriction that is always satisfied by standard distribution families, the confidence effect always dominates the cash augmentation effect: the optimal seller security is the least informationally sensitive security: seller debt.2

We believe that our setting is a reasonable approximation of the information environment in many asset acquisitions. First, the real-world relevance of asset compatibility risk is evidenced by acquirers devoting considerable effort and expense to contract around incompatibility risk via material adverse change (MAC) clauses.3However, these clauses are expensive to litigate (Elken 2008) and can only specify a subset of anticipated, and thus contractible, contingencies (Masulis and Simsir 2018). Other mechanisms, such as seller securities, are therefore required. Second, little public information is available about privately held assets. Thus, assuming that sellers have better information about the incompatibility risk than the prospective acquirer seems reasonable in this context. Third, assuming that the acquirer is more confident about the success of the value-add plan than the seller is consistent with the standard definitions of overconfidence and with evidence.4Yet, the approach we develop can be applied to settings that do not feature belief disagreement. We show that the same result is obtained with common beliefs but differing stochastic discount factors across agents (as assumed in, for exampleGârleanu, Panageas, and Yu 2015;Gomes and Michaelides 2008).

Implications:Our analysis reveals many implications that are relevant for offer designs in the asset acquisition market when target assets are controlled by a single owner. These implications can be conveniently divided into two categories: 1) general implications that provide a rational-choice security design foundation for the actual first-order characteristics of this large market, and, 2) comparative statics, that is, implications that relate model parameters to the structuring of specific offers.

The three general implications of our analysis are that (a) offers always feature some cash (specifically, target-performance insensitive cash equivalent considerations); (b) seller debt is the dominant security design for the performance sensitive component of the offers; and (c) even when acquirers are not cash constrained, they may prefer mixed cash and security offers to all-cash offers. As we discuss in detail in the literature review section, no existing security-design model entails these three implications.

These implications are consistent with the offer structures actually used in the asset acquisition market. Seller securities are never the only consideration offered to sellers and, typically, most of the consideration offered to sellers is in cash (including cash-equivalent payments such as buyer stocks). As discussed earlier, seller debt is the dominant form of performance-sensitive consideration in the market for privately held assets. It is prima facie implausible that the acquirers, who typically buy assets that are orders of magnitude smaller than themselves, offer seller debt only because they are unable to fund cash offers. Also, seller debt is nonrecourse and held by the seller rather than a third party.5Second, seller debt is never observed when the target is publicly traded.6

Our analysis also produces a rich set of comparative statics that relate our model parameters to offer design. For example, the acquirer ismorelikely to propose a combination of seller debt and cash (as opposed to an all-cash offer) when (a) a mismatch between the seller’s assets and the acquirer’s value-add plan ismorelikely; (b) the undervaluation of the seller debt claim is small, given the acquirer’s beliefs; and (c) the value of the asset undersellercontrol ismoresensitive to the seller’s private information.

Our analysis also has subtler implications. First, absent acquirer confidence, the acquirer never strictly prefers seller debt to other securities that screen out incompatible sellers. Hence, acquirer confidence is essential only if seller debt is to be the unique optimal security.

Second, when the confidence gap between the seller and the acquirer is large, the acquirer prefers not to relinquish any of the acquisition cash flows to the seller through security considerations and instead opts for an all-cash offer, even though all-cash offers do not screen out incompatible assets. Thus, offering seller debt (or any other seller security) is only optimal when there is some confidence gap, but only up to a point.

Third, in the absence of belief heterogeneity, the only motivation for offering a security consideration is to screen out incompatible sellers. In the absence of information asymmetry, confident acquirers never use security considerations. However, with belief heterogeneity and information asymmetry, confident acquirers sometimes prefer offers that (a) include security consideration and (b) do not screen out incompatible sellers.

Fourth, if the liquidity preference of the seller exceeds the liquidity preference of the acquirer and the acquirer has a competitive advantage in supplying liquidity to the seller, the optimal seller security is mezzanine seller debt (sandwiched between a debt claim and an equity claim). This case highlights the tension between the cash-augmentation effect favoring performance-sensitive seller securities and the confidence effect favoring performance-insensitive securities. The seller’s preference for liquidity boosts the cash-augmentation effect and thus favors more performance-sensitive seller securities.

Fifth, and finally, we show that our key results hold when extending the model to situations in which there is competition among buyers, and situations in which buyers have common beliefs but heterogeneous stochastic discount factors.
Our paper models the acquisition of non-public firms in an information-based security design framework. The novel feature of our model is that the beliefs of acquirers and sellers about future cash flows differ. When agents have common beliefs and the same information, they cannot “agree to disagree” (Aumann 1976). In our risk-neutral setting, in which priors are heterogeneous, differences in valuations are possible.

There appears to be no research that theoretically models the joint effects of belief differences and common priors in a corporate finance setting.7However, the specific form of belief disagreement we model, managerial overconfidence (or, more specifically, acquirer overconfidence), has been explored in the empirical literature (e.g.,Hirshleifer, Low, and Teoh 2012;Malmendier and Tate 2008). A line of theoretical literature considers the effects of heterogeneous priors (e.g.,Panageas 2020;Simsek 2013). In contrast to our setup that encompasses both agent heterogeneity and adverse selection, these papers focus on agent heterogeneity produced by either (a) heterogeneous stochastic discount factors and common prior beliefs or (b) homogeneous stochastic discount factors and heterogeneous beliefs.8

In many respects, our framework conforms with other models that aim to identify optimal security designs for raising capital in incomplete information settings (Nachman and Noe 1994;DeMarzo and Duffie 1999;Malenko and Tsoy 2020;Yang 2020). Our specification of the feasible set of securities is the same as in these models, and we study the allocation of cash flow rights rather than control rights. In contrast to these papers, we model asset acquisition rather than capital raising.

Within the class of capital-raising security design models, the belief specification ofMalenko and Tsoy (2020)is the closest to ours. In their model, uninformed security buyers use the worst-case beliefs mandated by Knightian uncertainty aversion to evaluate securities. As these beliefs usually differ for the security issuer and the buyer, their model features belief heterogeneity. However, their results are different from ours. In their setting, optimal securities provide the informed agent (the security issuer) with an equity (or levered equity) claim. This contrasts with our setting where the informed agent (the asset seller) never receives a seller equity claim. Moreover, key features of seller debt (e.g., being nonrecourse and issued to a specific agent) are irrelevant or difficult to define in their capital-raising model. In summary, it is difficult to compare their results to ours despite the similarity of the setup.9

Although security design is not the focus ofSimsek (2013), that paper does derive optimal security designs in a belief divergence framework characterizing how the interaction between collateral constraints and belief divergence affects asset prices.Simsek (2013)considers a world populated with optimistic and pessimistic investors and shows that when optimists’ beliefs are more “skewed to the upside,” the effect of belief divergence on asset prices is larger. We will further examine this issue in Section 4.6.

Our paper is also related to the common-priors, adverse-selection-based security design literature (e.g.,DeMarzo and Duffie 1999;Nachman and Noe 1994). Like these papers, we (a) provide conditions under which debt is the optimal security design and (b) impose stochastic ordering conditions which ensure that the least informationally sensitive security is debt.10However, our results and economic framework differ substantially from these papers. These papers model settings where security issuance by informed parties is motivated by liquidity preferences or financial constraints. Our paper models the incentives of uninformed and financially unconstrained agents to use security consideration. In addition, the adverse-selection, common-priors security design literature provides conditions under which it is optimal to allocate the least informationally sensitive security to the uninformed party. Our analysis provides conditions under which it is optimal to assign the least informationally sensitive security to theinformed party, that is, the seller.

With respect to the economic framework, the common-prior-beliefs security design framework inDeMarzo, Kremer, and Skrzypacz (2005)is closest to ours. In an auction setting, where competing informed buyers make offers to an uninformed seller,DeMarzo, Kremer, and Skrzypacz (2005)model bidding with securities. Buyers bid by offering seller security claims on the cash flow produced by the auctioned good.11The similarity with our study arises when their model is reinterpreted as one of competing buyers for an asset.DeMarzo, Kremer, and Skrzypacz (2005)model two mechanisms. First, under the seller screening mechanism, issuing a debt claim to the seller is actually the worst offer design. The second mechanism is a buyer signaling mechanism. If the acquirer is completely financially constrained, the optimal security design is debt issued to the seller (Proposition 5). Thus, their signaling mechanism can only explain the use of seller debt if all competing acquirers have no pledgeable assets (including cash and shares). In the context of private firm acquisitions, however, acquirers do have pledgeable assets. In all acquisition offers, there is a nonzero cash-equivalent component. Thus, theDeMarzo, Kremer, and Skrzypacz (2005)model, reinterpreted as a model of seller security–financed asset acquisitions, does not provide a rationale for why seller-debt financing offers are observed.12Thus, our framework appears to be the only extant framework that rationalizes a first-order characteristic of the asset acquisition market: the use of mixed seller debt and cash offers when financing constraints are not binding.13

In our model, the right combination of cash and security consideration is an important component of the offer design. Many researchers have analyzed the choice between cash and acquirer equity considerations in mergers, but they have focused on public targets, where selling shareholders never receive a debt claim. In addition, they focus on acquirer equity, not seller securities, as a means of payment (Eckbo, Giammarino, and Heinkel 1990;Rhodes-Kropf and Viswanathan 2004;Shleifer and Vishny 2003;Officer 2004;Gorbenko and Malenko 2017;Liu and Bernhardt 2021).

In a related paper,Fishman (1989)solves a model of preemptive bidding in acquisitions of public firms. The key difference between his model and ours is thatFishman (1989)assumes that the acquirer knows the payoff from the acquisition conditioned on seller type (compatible versus incompatible) and any seller debt that is issued to finance the acquisition will default with probability 1 if the assets are incompatible. Thus, absent potential rival acquirers, all-seller debt offers are optimal security designs, and cash offers are never optimal. Importantly,Fishman (1989)acknowledges that seller debt in his setting is equivalent to contracting directly on the seller’s private information. This setting differs from our setting, in which acquirers and sellers do not know the future payoffs but instead rely on noisy signals of future payoffs. Moreover,Fishman’s (1989)prediction that optimal offers have no cash component, contrasts with our prediction, all optimal offers have a positive cash component. In the asset acquisition market, whichFishman (1989)was not attempting to model, all offer have a cash component.
article.2 Preliminaries:
2.1 Formal setup

We consider the problem of an acquirer (she),A⁠, structuring the purchase of control rights over an asset from a seller (he),S⁠. Both the seller and the acquirer are risk-neutral and have a discount rate of zero. The acquirer has a value-add plan for an asset owned by the seller. The seller knows whether his asset is compatible with the acquirer’s value-add plan. In stateC⁠, the “compatible” state, his asset is compatible. In stateI⁠, his asset is not compatible. Only the seller knows the state (⁠IorC⁠). The acquirer’s prior probability distribution over the two states assigns probabilityππto the state’s beingC⁠. Negotiations between the acquirer and the seller occur at date 0 and concern the sale of the asset whosecash flowsare all realized at date 1.14

Conditioned on control being transferred to the acquirer, the distribution of cash flows at date 1 isFij⁠,i,=S,A⁠,j=C,I⁠, wherejrepresents the state of the world andirepresents one of the parties to the transaction. For example,FACrepresents the acquirer’s distribution of cash flows conditioned on control being transferred and the state beingC⁠. Thus, the acquirer and seller have different subjective probability measures over the distribution of cash flows from the asset if control is transferred to the acquirer.

Conditioned on control being retained by the seller, we represent the distribution of cash flows by H i j ⁠ , i , = S , A ⁠ , j = C , I ⁠ . For example, H S C represents the seller’s distribution of cash flow conditioned on the seller retaining control of the asset and the state being C ⁠ . Given risk neutrality, the seller’s reservation payoff from rejecting an offer from the acquirer equals the seller’s expected payoff from the asset under seller control. We represent this payoff by R S i ⁠ , j = I , C ⁠ , that is, R S j = ∫ x d H S j ( x ) .

Similarly, when control is transferred, we represent the expected cash flow , given state j ⁠ , j = C , I ⁠ , given the beliefs of i ⁠ , i = A , S by μ μ i j ⁠ , with μ μ i j = ∫ x d F i j ( x ) .

At date 0, the acquirer makes a first-and-final offer to the seller which, if accepted, transfers control of the asset from the acquirer to the seller. The first-and-final offer mechanism implies that the acquirer possesses the bargaining power in the negotiation, which is consistent with the bargaining-power assumption that is typically used in the acquisition literature (Hansen 1987;Fishman 1989). Although assigning the bargaining power to the seller (by assuming that the seller moves first) would make our analysis incompatible with most of the literature on acquisitions, it would not rule out the optimality of seller debt (see Section 5 for a discussion andInternet Appendix Ffor an example).

The acquirer offers a package consisting of a cash paymentp≥0and a securitys⁠. The cash payment is made at date 0. The security is a claim on the date 1 cash flows produced by the asset. If the offer is accepted, control of the asset is transferred to the acquirer, and the seller receives a cash payment ofp≥0at date 0 and the security payoffsat date 1. “Cash,” as defined in the model, is intended to represent any cash equivalent consideration transferred from the acquirer to the seller at date 0. Thus, in our baseline model, the acquirer is not cash constrained.15

At date 1, the cash flowxis realized. If the offer was rejected at date 0, the entire cash flow accrues to the seller. If the offer was accepted, the seller receives a payment ofs(x)and the acquirer receivesx−s(x)⁠. Thus, the distribution of cash flows depends on (a) the identity of the agent (acquirerAor sellerS⁠), (b) the state of the world (⁠CorI⁠), and (c) whether control is transferred.

We impose the standard assumptions in the security design literature on the admissible set of securities, that is, we assumemonotonicity(⁠x,→s(x)is nondecreasing, andx→x−s(x)is nondecreasing) andlimited liability(⁠0≤s(x)≤x⁠) (e.g.,Nachman and Noe 1994;DeMarzo and Duffie 1999;Axelson 2007). We assume that all distributions—Fij⁠,Hij⁠,i=S,A⁠,j=C,I—have finite expectations, are supported byR+⁠, and have continuous densities that are positive over(0,∞)⁠. Note that in our setting there is no riskless component of cash flows. Such a component would have no effect on our analysis because its value does not vary with the acquirer’s beliefs or the seller’s private information.

2.2 Security density

The monotonicity restrictions on s imply that for any two distinct cash flows, x ′ and x ″ ⁠ , such that x ″ > x ′ ⁠ , it must be the case that 0 ≤ s ( x ″ ) − s ( x ′ ) ≤ x ″ − x ′ ⁠ . That is, the slope of s is bounded between 0 and 1 ⁠ . These conditions imply that s is absolutely continuous and that s has a density. This density is a measurable function almost everywhere equal to s ′ ⁠ . We call this security density w ⁠ , where w ( x ) ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] ⁠ . As limited liability implies that s ( 0 ) = 0 ⁠ , a security’s payoff can be recovered from its density w as follows: s ( x ) = ∫ 0 x w ( t ) d t .

Using security densities makes characterizing optimal security designs more tractable. These densities can be heuristically interpreted as marginal allocations of cash flows. If the cash flow equalsx⁠,w(x)represents the proportion of cash flows betweenxandx+dx(⁠dx≈0⁠) allocated to the seller. A debt security allocates all marginal cash flows to the seller,w=1⁠, until cash flows equal the debt face value, at which point the security allocates no marginal cash flows to the sellerw=0⁠.

Note that the value of a security under a given distribution of cash flows F equals ∫ 0 ∞ s ( x ) d F ( x ) .

Result B-1 in Internet Appendix B shows that we can express the value of a security s under distribution function F in terms of the security density and complementary distribution function, F ¯ = 1 − F ⁠ , via the identity ∫ 0 ∞ s ( x ) d F ( x ) = ∫ 0 ∞ w ( x ) F ¯ ( x ) d x . (1)

2.3 Stochastic orderings of cash flows

In our analysis, one distribution of cash flows is considered “better” than another if it dominates the other distribution in the monotone likelihood ratio (MLR) ordering (Milgrom 1981).

A distributionMLR(strictly)dominatesanother distribution if the ratio between the probability density functions of the dominant,f⁠, and dominated,g⁠, distributions is (strictly) increasing. In our analysis, the key implication of MLR dominance used in our analysis is Result 1:

Result 1 ( theorem 1 .C.1 in Shaked and Shanthikumar (2007) ). If F and G are any two absolutely continuous distributions supported by [ 0 , ∞ ) ⁠ , and F strictly MLR dominates G ⁠ , then x , → F ¯ ( x ) / G ¯ ( x ) is strictly increasing for x > 0 and equals one at x = 0 ⁠ . 18

Remark 2. Note also that if X ˜ ∼ d F and Y ˜ ∼ d G and the map x , → F ¯ ( x ) / G ¯ ( x ) is strictly increasing, then [ X ˜ | X > t ] first-order stochastically dominates [ Y ˜ | Y > t ] for all t > 0 ⁠ . ( theorem 1 .B.7 Shaked and Shanthikumar 2007 ). Hence, MLR ordering implies not only first-order stochastic dominance, but also that the distribution of the dominant random variable, conditioned on cash flows lying in the upside interval [ t , ∞ ) ⁠ , is first-order stochastically dominant. In this sense, MLR ordering implies “upside dominance.”

Note that a distributionasymptotically dominatesanother if the density ratio converges to infinity as the cash flow at which the densities are evaluated converges to infinity. MLR dominance implies asymptotic dominance for many commonly employed distributions (e.g., the lognormal, Gamma, Exponential, Weibull). Applying l’Hôpital rule shows that asymptotic dominance implies thatF¯(x)/G¯(x)→∞asx→∞⁠.

article.3 Two Forces: Belief Divergence and Asym:
Our paper analyzes how the interaction between two forces—acquirer/seller belief divergence and seller private information—shapes the design of the securities used to finance private-firm acquisitions. To make the subsequent analysis of this interaction more intuitive, we first discuss how each force acts in isolation.

3.1 Only belief divergence

Suppose that we turn off the effect of asymmetric information and consider a world where the probability that the asset is compatible,ππ⁠, equals one. Thus, it is common knowledge that the asset value under acquirer control exceeds its value under seller control. The confident acquirer’s objective is maximizing the gains from trade which, in our setting, reduces to minimizing the acquirer’s valuation of consideration offered to the seller subject to the consideration satisfying the seller’s reservation demand.

Given this objective, consider the effects of security design. As explained inRemark 1, the contribution to meeting the seller’s reservation demand from offering a marginal $1 atxisF¯SC(x)⁠. The cost to the acquirer of offering the seller a marginal $1 atxisF¯AC(x)⁠. The cost/contribution ratio of offering marginal cashxto the seller is thusF¯AC(x)/F¯SC(x)⁠. This ratio measures the degree of belief divergence between the more confident acquirer and less confident seller about whether cash flows will exceedx⁠.

The acquirer aims to minimize the cost/contribution ratio. Even a very weak definition of acquirer confidence, strict first-order stochastic dominance of the acquirer’s cash flow distribution over the seller’s, ensures thatF¯AC(x)/F¯SC(x)>1⁠. Because the acquirer’s and seller’s valuations of cash are the same, the cost/contribution ratio for cash consideration is one. So, the acquirer always offers all-cash consideration and hence offers never include any security consideration.

If the acquirer were cash constrained, that is, could not offer cash consideration, the acquirer would choose the security that offers marginal dollars at the cash flows that have the lowest cost/contribution ratio,F¯AC(x)/F¯SC(x)⁠. If acquirer confidence is defined by MLR dominance of the acquirer’s cash flow distribution, Result 1 shows that the cost/contribution ratio is increasing. Thus, the acquirer will allocate all of the lower marginal cash flows to the seller and none of the higher marginal cash flows, that is, the acquirer would always finance the acquisition with a seller debt security.19

3.2 Only asymmetric information

Now suppose that acquirers are not more confident than sellers; that is, the acquirer and the seller agree on the distributions of cash flows post acquisition when the asset is compatible or incompatible. A compatible asset under seller control is more valuable than an incompatible asset under seller control but less valuable than a compatible asset under acquirer control. When the asset is incompatible, it is common knowledge that the value of the asset is independent of whether the asset is acquired.

In this case, acquirer’s objective is to screen out sellers with incompatible assets and minimize the information rents captured by sellers with compatible assets. Any all-security offer can screen out sellers with incompatible assets. To see this, note that when the asset is incompatible, the seller’s reservation demand is the value of the asset under seller control, which equals the value of the asset under acquirer control. If the acquirer makes a pure security offer, the cash flow to the seller from the seller security written on the asset is never greater and sometimes less than the cash flow of the asset itself. Thus, a seller with incompatible assets will reject any pure security offer.

Conditioned on the offer only being accepted by the sellers with compatible assets, the acquirer and seller have common beliefs about the value of all securities written on the asset. Thus, the acquirer is indifferent between all securities that provide the same value to the seller. Hence, any pure security offer that exactly satisfies the seller reservation demand, that is, whose value equals the value of a compatible asset under seller control, is optimal. Because, sellers with incompatible offers will accept any all-cash offer that sellers with compatible assets will accept, all-cash offers that result in the acquisition of compatible assets always also result in acquiring incompatible assets worth less than the consideration offered. Hence, all-cash offers are never optimal. Because, optimal pure security offers always exist, in this setting, if cash constraints were imposed, they would have no effect on the welfare of either the seller or acquirer.
article.4 The Baseline Model:
As shown in Section 3, when beliefs are heterogeneous but information is symmetric, acquirers strictly prefer all-cash offers. Thus, offering seller debt consideration is never optimal. When information is asymmetric but beliefs are homogeneous, all-cash offers are never optimal and seller debt is never the only optimal form of security consideration. We now show that when beliefs are heterogeneousandinformation is asymmetric, seller debt is the uniquely optimal form for security consideration, all-cash offers are sometimes optimal, and all optimal offers feature some cash consideration.

4.1 Assumptions

We first provide our set of assumptions, which we believe are quite weak.Assumption 1states that stateCis unambiguously better than stateIfor both parties in the sense of MLR ordering.

Assumption 1. Cash flows in the two states satisfy the following ordering conditions: F A C strictly MLR and asymptotically dominates F A I ⁠ ; F S C strictly MLR and asymptotically dominates F S I ⁠ ; μ μ S C > R S C > R S I > 0 ⁠ . The following two assumptions are key to our framework. Assumption 2 states that both parties agree that an acquisition in state C adds value, but the acquirer is more confident about her value-add capacity than the seller. Although MLR ordering is a standard assumption, defining acquirer confidence in terms of MLR ordering does have a significant effect on our results. This effect can best be understood after we develop these results, so we defer further discussion of these implications to Section 4.6.

Assumption 2. F A C strictly MLR and asymptotically dominates F S C ⁠ . The meaning of Assumption 3 is that the acquirer’s value-add plan depends on the target asset having specific characteristics known only by the seller. In state C ⁠ , the asset has the characteristics necessary for value creation, but in state I it does not. Hence, in state I ⁠ , the acquisition is a zero NPV project. For example, suppose an acquirer seeks to acquire a lab that produces medical devices. The acquirer believes her superior distribution network can expand the market reach of the device, but this synergy will only manifest if the target’s product is reliable—information that only the seller knows.

Assumption 3. F A I = F S I and μ μ S I = R S I ⁠ ; As discussed in Section 4.7, all of the results go through under the more standard assumption that μ μ S I < R S I ( Fishman 1989 ), that is, it is common knowledge that the acquisition reduces value when the value-add plan is not compatible with the seller’s assets, and also under the assumption that the value added by incompatible assets is positive but sufficiently small. The advantage of imposing Assumption 3 in the baseline setting is that it captures our parsimonious narrative: the only channel through which the acquisition affects the seller’s assets, and the only source of disagreement between the acquirer and seller, is the acquirer’s value-add plan.

Finally, the only additional distributional restriction we impose is on the tail ratios (Definition 3):

Assumption 4 (ratio condition). r A ′ ( x ) / r C ′ ( x ) is strictly increasing in x ⁠ . Assumption 4 is a “technical assumption” (to supply one hypothesis to Lemma C-1 in the Internet Appendix ) that is required for producing determinant predictions about the optimal security designs in our setting (which features both asymmetric information and belief divergence). We defer discussion of the implications of this assumption to the next section. However, it is worthwhile to note that Assumption 4 does not significantly curtail the scope of our analysis; for textbook distribution families supported by the nonnegative real line (e.g., lognormal, Exponential, Gamma), Assumption 4 is satisfied whenever Assumptions 1 and 2 are satisfied (see Internet Appendix E ). The only role played by Assumption 4 in our analysis is that Assumption 4 is a necessary and sufficient condition for establishing Lemma C-1 in Internet Appendix Section C.1 . 4.2 Solving for the optimal separating offer

A separating offer is an offer the seller is willing to accept if the state is C ⁠ , and is willing to reject if the state is I ⁠ ; a separating offer is a solution to the following problem: π μ Max w ∈ W , p ∈ R π ( μ A C − ( v A C ( w ) + p ) ) , IC - C: v S C ( w ) + p ≥ R S C , IC - I - s: v S I ( w ) + p ≤ R S I , SLL : p ≥ 0. The IC constraints (IC-C and IC-I-s) ensure that the separating offer is incentive compatible. SLL ensures that the seller limited liability constraint is satisfied.

We start with a simple result: the incentive compatibility constraint when the assets are compatible, IC-C, always binds. The intuition for this result is that if IC-C does not bind, the acquirer can make the offer less attractive to the seller by reducing the cash or the security component of the offer. This reduces the seller’s incentive to accept the offer in the incompatible stateIand lowers the cost of the acquisition borne by the acquirer. If the reduction is sufficiently small, the IC-C constraint is still satisfied. Because the seller’s valuation of an optimal offer when the asset is compatible equals his reservation, and the asset is not acquired when the asset is incompatible, the seller earns no information rents.

Lemma 1. Suppose Assumptions 1 and 2 are satisfied and ( w , p ) is an optimal solution to problem (P-Sep), then IC-C binds, that is, v S C ( w ) + p = R S C ⁠ .

The next lemma shows that all optimal separating offers contain a security component and a cash component. The logic for the inclusion of a security component is straightforward: because the seller’s reservation value when assets are incompatible (⁠RSI⁠) is lower than the seller’s reservation value when assets are compatible (⁠RSC⁠), then any all-cash offer that the seller is weakly willing to accept in stateCwould be strictly accepted in stateI⁠. So, an all-cash offer cannot separate.

The logic behind optimal separating offers having a cash component is subtle: a zero-cash offer only promises the seller a claim on the cash flows from the acquired asset. A seller security that is secured only by the acquired assets must be worth less than the acquired asset itself. When the state isI⁠, the acquisition does not add value (Assumption 3). Thus, the assets’ value under seller control (which the seller receives if he rejects the offer) is always greater than the value of the acquisition offer. Hence, if the offer is a zero-cash offer, the incentive constraint in stateI⁠, IC-I-s, does not bind. In this case, the acquirer can substitute some cash for the security in a fashion that leaves the incentive compatibility constraint in stateC(IC-C) satisfied. A sufficiently small substitution of security for cash consideration will leave the slack incentive constraint (IC-I-s) satisfied and will reduce the cost of the acquisition because the acquirer’s valuation of the seller security claim on the acquired assets is higher than that of the seller. (Assumption 2).

Proposition 2 and Remark 3 show that in pooling solutions as in separating solutions, sellers with compatible assets earn no information rents. However, unlike in the only-asymmetric-information case, when the asset is incompatible the seller does earn an information rent, that is, v S I ( w ) + p > R S I ⁠ . The information rents to sellers with incompatible assets result from belief divergence. Information rents can be reduced by increasing the offer’s security component. However, because of acquirer confidence, the acquirer values the security component more than the seller does when the asset is compatible. The belief divergence costs of increasing the security component of the offer can exceed the gain from reducing the information rents.

Cash consideration generates no belief divergence costs. Yet,Proposition 2does not preclude the use of security consideration in pooling offers. The logic supporting optimal pooling offers containing a security component is subtle. To gain some intuition, consider the effect of reducing the cash component of the all-cash offer by $1 and using seller debt instead. When the asset iscompatible, the seller participation constraint is binding. Thus, the seller debt should be worth $1 to a seller, but it is worth more than $1 to the acquirer. When the asset isincompatible, the seller debt replacing $1 of cash consideration is worth less than $1 to the seller. As the seller’s and the acquirer’s valuations are the same in this case (Assumption 3), it is also worth less than $1 to the acquirer. Hence, adding security consideration to a pooling offer increases the acquisition cost when the asset is compatible and decreases the acquisition cost when the asset is incompatible. If the probability of the asset being compatible is sufficiently small, a pooling offer with a security component dominates an all-cash pooling offer.21

Given that security consideration is sometimes optimal in pooling offers, why is it optimal for security consideration to take the form of seller debt? In the separating problem, the Lagrange multiplier associated with the mimicry constraining,λλC⁠, imposes a penalty ofλλIon every dollar of value received by the seller with incompatible assets. In this way, the multiplier ensures that the solution satisfies the nonmimicry condition, IC:I-p. In the pooling problem, this constraint is absent. Instead, the fact that a seller with incompatible assets accepts the offer imposes, on the acquirer, a penalty ofπ1−πon every dollar of value received by the seller. Proposition 1’s verification of the optimality of seller debt does not depend on the specific value of the penalty. Thus, Assumptions 1–4, which ensure the optimality of seller debt in separating problems, also ensure the optimality of seller debt in pooling problems.

4.5 Interpretations

4.5.1 Use of seller debt financing

The question of whether using some seller debt is optimal reduces to the question of whether the derivative of the payoff function is positive for debt issues with sufficiently small face values. The answer to this question is affirmative if and only if condition (b) ofTheorem 1is satisfied.

The left-hand side of condition (b) is the limit of a ratio as k → 0 ⁠ . The numerator of the ratio, F ¯ A C ( k ) − F ¯ S C ( k ) ⁠ , captures the acquirer’s relative confidence about the cash flows from implementing the value-add plan. The denominator of the ratio, F ¯ S C ( k ) − F ¯ S I ( k ) ⁠ , captures the sensitivity of the seller’s valuation of the asset to compatibility. Because asset compatibility is privately observed by the seller, F ¯ S C ( k ) − F ¯ S I ( k ) measures the effect of private information. The right-hand side of equation 13 is the probability of being in state I and captures the risk of acquiring an incompatible asset. Thus, we can interpret condition (b) of the theorem as follows: π π lim k → 0 + F ¯ A C ( k ) − F ¯ S C ( k ) ︷ acq . confidence F ¯ S C ( k ) − F ¯ S I ( k ) ︸ asymmetric information < 1 − π π } mismatch risk . (13)

Using this interpretation, we see that the ratio, around the lowest possible cash flows, between the acquirer confidence and asymmetric information effects is sufficiently small, or mismatching risk is sufficiently large, that the acquirer will prefer using some seller debt to finance the offer.

Note again that the satisfaction of the conditions for the optimality of seller debt relative to other seller securities does not depend on the relative magnitude of the acquirer confidence and asymmetric information effects. However,Theorem 1shows that the mix between seller debt and cash does depend on the magnitude of the acquirer confidence relative to asymmetric information effects.

4.6.1Assumption 1

To distill the notion that both the seller and acquirer believe that cash flows when the asset is compatible (information signalC⁠) are “larger” than cash flows when the asset is incompatible (information signalI⁠),Assumption 1defines “larger” as “MLR dominant.” Within agency theory and information economics, MLR ordering is, by a wide margin, the most commonly employed stochastic ordering relation. In contrast,Nachman and Noe (1994)assume that types are ordered by what they call “conditional stochastic dominance” but is more commonly termed “hazard-rate-order dominance” (HRO). Conditional stochastic dominance simply means that the tail ratio between the cash flow distributions of a “better” and a “worse” type is increasing. Result 1 shows that MLR ordering implies that the tail ratios,rAandrC⁠, are increasing. As noted above, in our setting the properties of the tail ratios are the only properties of the cash flow distribution that affect security design. Thus, in our context these two approaches are equivalent.

4.6.2Assumption 2

Assumption 2imposes MLR ordering on the beliefs of the acquirer and the seller with respect to the post-acquisition cash flows resulting from compatible assets. Relative to the number of asymmetric information models, there are few models of belief divergence. However, in a security design setting, Simsek (assumption A2 2013) employs an ordering condition which ensures that tail ratios are increasing.Simsek (2013)follows the approach ofNachman and Noe (1994)and assumes that optimists’ beliefs hazard-rate-order-dominate the pessimists’ beliefs.

Assumption s 1 and 2 ensure that the distributions we consider satisfy the distributional restrictions of all of the security design models discussed above. Thus, our perhaps surprising result—that the optimal security design offers the informed party (the seller) the least informationally sensitive security (seller debt)—cannot be attributed to our considering distributions that fail to satisfy the ordering conditions typically imposed in security design models (cf. Fulghieri, Garcia, and Hackbarth 2020 ).

The restriction on the tail ratios imposed by MLR dominance ordering (Assumptions 1 and 2)—namely, thatrAandrCare strictly increasing (see Result 1)—is essential for establishing our results. As we pointed out inRemark 2, MLR ordering entails upside-conditioned dominance: conditioned on cash flows exceeding any fixed threshold,t>0⁠, the acquirer’s distribution of post-acquisition cash flows of compatible assets,[X˜AC|X˜AC>t]⁠, first-order stochastically dominates the seller’s distribution,[X˜SC|X˜SC>t]⁠.22InInternet Appendix C.5, we provide an example in which the acquirer’s distribution of post-acquisition cash flows of compatible assets first-order stochastically dominates the seller’s distribution but is not upside-conditioned dominant. In this example, seller levered equity is the optimal security design.

4.7 The role of seller private information about compatibility

Assumption 3 posits that the informed party (the seller) has private information about whether transacting with the uninformed counterparty (the acquirer) creates value, or, more specifically in our context, whether the acquired asset is compatible with the acquirer’s value-add plan. When the asset is incompatible with the plan, no economic gains are created by the acquirer and seller transacting.

Models in which the informed party has private information about whether a transaction will generate economic value are ubiquitous in the economics and finance literature. In acquisition models, for example, the sellers have private information indicating that an acquisition will not generate value (Fishman 1989). In the credit rationing and debt–equity choice settings, security issuers have negative NPV projects (e.g.,Biais and Gollier 1997;Fulghieri and Lukin 2001;Cooney and Kalay 1993). And in venture capital settings, VCs who might be fly-by-night operators cannot add value (Axelson, Strömberg, and Weisbach 2009). All of these papers model environments in which information in general is opaque and where any information on whether the transaction will generate value is “soft”; that is, even when revealed to the uninformed party ex post, it is not verifiable and thus not contractible.

The role ofAssumption 3, which relates to the effect of asset incompatibility, is subtle. As we discuss below,Assumption 3indirectly operates on optimal security design through its interaction with the seller limited liability constraint (SLL).

Cash payments, because they are insensitive to seller private information, encourage sellers of incompatible assets to accept acquirer offers. So, for any given seller security component of an offer, there exists a maximum cash component (possibly negative) such that, for any larger cash payment, the offer will not satisfy the incentive constraints for separation.

The maximum cash component of offers consistent with separation is smaller for seller debt offers than for any alternative seller security. However, the cash-minimizing feature of seller debt will affect the optimality of seller debt in the baseline model only if either the maximum feasible cash component of seller debt offers is zero and the seller limited liability constraint (SLL) is binding, or the maximum feasible cash component in seller debt offers is negative and seller debt offers violate the SLL constraint. Thus, the cash-minimizing feature of seller debt does not affect the optimality of seller debt if:

(*) there exist seller debt-plus-cash offers that implement separation and exactly satisfy the seller’s reservation demand when assets are compatible,RSC⁠.Condition (*) is satisfied if and only ifwheneverDSC(k)=RSC,DSI(k)<RSI.(16)

Assumption 3 posits weak incompatibility: acquiring the seller’s asset in the state I ⁠ , when the assets are incompatible with the value-add plan, is a zero NPV transaction; that is, μ μ S I = R S I ⁠ . In this case, because a debt claim on an asset is worth less than the asset itself, it is obvious that μ D S I ( k ) < μ S I = R S I and thus Condition (*) is satisfied. However, any assumption that verifies (*) is sufficient for establishing our mathematical results and thus for verifying the optimality of seller debt. It is common knowledge that, in state I ⁠ , acquisition destroys value (c.f. Fishman 1989 ); that is, μ μ μ A I = μ S I < R S I ⁠ . Thus, strong incompatibility also ensures that Condition (*) is satisfied. Even if the acquisition increases value somewhat in state I —i.e., if μ μ μ A I = μ S I > R S I —Condition (*) can be satisfied. For example, as shown in Internet Appendix C , if asset cash flows are exponentially distributed, equation (16) and thus Condition (*) is satisfied (assuming Assumption 1 holds) if and only if μ μ μ μ μ μ ( μ S C − R S C μ S C ) μ S C > ( μ S I − R S I μ S I ) μ S I . Thus, our results establishing the optimality of seller debt (Propositions 1 and 2) under the weak incompatibility (Assumption 3) are robust to replacing this assumption with strong incompatibility and even a limited degree of compatibility.Assumption 3is not adeus ex machinasummoned to provide a desideratum to verify optimality of seller debt, but rather a natural assumption about the effect of acquisitions on incompatible assets in a framework where all economic frictions center on the value-add plan.
article.5 Extensions:
5.1 Cash-constrained acquirers

Given that acquirers are typically orders of magnitude larger than sellers, the notion that sellers have a comparative advantage in providing capital for the acquisition seems unrealistic. However, analytically, it is easy to extend our analysis to settings in which the acquirer’s access to cash or cash equivalent compensation is limited and thus the acquirer must rely, to some extent, on financing provided by the seller. We note that cash constrained is a strong assumption, implying that acquirers can neither raise the desired capital from external lenders nor use their stocks as payment consideration. Nevertheless, if the acquirer is cash constrained, the amount of cash consideration that the acquirer can offer the seller is limited by an upper bound,p¯⁠, wherep¯>0but arbitrarily small. In this setting, the essential results of our analysis are robust.

Result 3. As in the baseline model of cash unconstrained acquirers, when acquirers are cash constrained, optimal offers always feature a positive cash component and, if an optimal offer features a security component, the security component is seller debt. Result 3 should not be too surprising. When the acquirer is cash constrained, the acquirer is forced to substitute security consideration for the cash consideration she would have offered absent financial constraints. Within the set of feasible security designs, the seller debt security is the closest substitute for cash. Thus, cash constraints simply provide another motivation for offering seller debt.

5.2 Common beliefs with heterogeneous stochastic discount factors

In our model, the acquirer and seller have different prior beliefs about the cash flows that would result from implementing the acquirer’s value-add plan, but both parties still value cash flows the same way: by taking expectations. Although differences in valuations are essential to our analysis, these differences need not be founded on heterogeneous prior beliefs. Different valuations can also arise from heterogeneous stochastic discount factors (SDFs), as featured in for example,Gârleanu, Panageas, and Yu (2015),Gomes and Michaelides (2008),Krusell and Smith (1998), andPanageas (2020).24

Consider a common prior/heterogeneous SDF valuation setting. Let the risk-free rate be zero. An SDF is a random variable, m ˜ ⁠ , that is positive with a probability of one and whose expected value, E [ m ˜ ] ⁠ , equals one. X ˜ j ⁠ , j = I , C represent project cash flows when the acquired asset is incompatible, I ⁠ , and compatible, C ⁠ , respectively. The distributions of random variables representing cash flows are the same for both agents. Let m ˜ i ⁠ , i = A , S ⁠ , represent the stochastic discount factors used by the agents. Under these assumptions, the value assigned to seller security, s ⁠ , by agent i = A , S in state j = I , C is given by E [ s ( X j ˜ ) m ˜ i ] ⁠ . The law of iterated expectations implies that E [ s ( X j ˜ ) m ˜ i ] = E [ s ( X j ˜ ) E [ m ˜ i | X j ˜ ] ] ⁠ . Thus, the value assigned to the security s by agent i in state j ⁠ , V i j ⁠ , is can be expressed as follows: V i j ( s ) : = E [ s ( X j ˜ ) E [ m ˜ i | X j ˜ ] ] , i = A , S , j = I , C . (18)

Using equation (18) , we can translate valuations provided by the heterogeneous SDFs and common priors into valuations produced by heterogeneous priors (and risk neutral valuation) as follows: V i j ( s ) = ∫ 0 ∞ s ( x ) f i j ( x ) d x , where f i j ( x ) : = E [ m ˜ i | X j ˜ = x ] f j ( x ) , and F i j ( x ) : = ∫ 0 x f i j ( t ) d t , i = A , S , j = I , C . (19)

Thus, for any specification of the SDFs in the heterogeneous SDF/common priors setting, there exist corresponding heterogeneous priors cash flow distributionsFji⁠. Whenever these cash flow distributions satisfy our distributional conditions (Assumptions 1–4), the common priors/heterogeneous SDF setting produces the same optimal security designs as our heterogeneous priors baseline model. Thus, when Assumptions 1–4 are satisfied by the corresponding heterogeneous prior distributions, the model can be interpreted as one where agents have common prior beliefs.

InInternet Appendix Section E, we show that when cash flows and SDFs are lognormally distributed (which is a ubiquitous parametric specification in asset-pricing models), simple parametric restrictions on the log means and log standard deviations of cash flows, and on the log covariance between cash flows and the SDF, can ensure the satisfaction of Assumptions 1–4. However, because little, if any, corporate finance research has been developed in a heterogeneous SDFs setting, finding an economic rationale for this specification is challenging. In contrast, several explanations for heterogeneous prior beliefs have been developed and tested in the literature. This is why we chose to use heterogeneous priors as a baseline model.

5.3 Impatient sellers

What happens when the acquirer’s marginal rate of substitution between date 0 cash and date 1 cash is less than the seller’s?25In the baseline model, the acquirer derives no benefit per se from compensating the seller with date 0 cash payments rather than date 1 security payments. However, if the seller is impatient, the acquirer will prefer mezzanine security designs.

The proof of this result is provided inInternet Appendix D.3, but we can quickly grasp the intuition underlying it. Offers featuring information-sensitive (information-insensitive) securities also feature more (less) cash consideration. The acquirer’s tradeoff is that, abstracting from the adverse selection problem caused by the seller’s private information, she prefers information-insensitive securities over information-sensitive securities, and cash consideration over security consideration. In the baseline setting, where the acquirer’s marginal rate of substitution between date 0 and date 1 cash is the same as the seller’s, this tension resolves in favor of minimizing information sensitivity at the cost of minimizing cash consideration by employing seller debt.

When the seller is impatient, the acquirer can profitably provide liquidity to him. Thus, seller impatience favors acquirer offers that feature more (date 0) cash consideration and less, but more information-sensitive, (date 1) security consideration. The lowest cash flows allocated to the seller under the seller debt contract are the cash flows least sensitive to the seller’s private information and to differences between seller and acquirer valuations based on acquirer confidence. As a result, when sellers are impatient and when acquirer offers feature a security component, the optimal security design is mezzanine debt, that is, a debt claim sandwiched between a risky debt claim and the acquirer’s equity claim.26Hence, our model predicts that when the demand for liquidity by asset owners is relatively high, seller debt tranches will be smaller and more junior (and will thus have higher interest rates).

5.4 Acquirer competition

How do the results of our model differ if, instead of a single acquirer making a first-and-final offer, a number of potential acquirers propose competing offers? We address this question inInternet Appendix Section 5.4by analyzing competition between potential acquirers who differ with respect to their degree of confidence. If sellers had no private information, introducing competition into this setting would be a trivial exercise. However, in our model, sellers do have private information. Thus, introducing competition transforms our model into a competitive screening model, that is, a model where uninformed agents compete by making offers to informed agents. Competitive screening does introduce some complications (detailed inInternet Appendix Section D.4) that are essentially orthogonal to the issues heretofore considered in our paper.

Using the simplest commonly employed framework for modeling competitive screening, we show that competition has a profound, but anticipated, effect on the distribution of rents between the potential acquirers and the seller. In the baseline model, a seller with compatible assets never earns rents (Lemmas 1 and 3). In the competitive screening setting, the payoff of a seller with compatible assets is never less than the seller’s estimate of post-acquisition asset value, which is greater than the seller’s reservation payoff (Assumption 1) Thus, competition ensures a seller with compatible assetsalwaysearns rents; in some cases, the seller receivesmorethan his estimate of the post-acquisition asset value. As in the baseline model, a seller with incompatible assets may or may not earn rents.

However, competition has no effect on optimal security design: all security consideration included in accepted acquirer offers takes the form of seller debt. Introducing competition only affects the mix between cash and seller debt. The robustness of our security design results is not surprising. In contrast to informed acquirer settings (e.g.,DeMarzo, Kremer, and Skrzypacz 2005), in our uninformed acquirer setting, the only channel through which rival offers affect other potential acquirers is through their effects on the minimum offer value required to secure offer acceptance. Two offers by a single rival acquirer which have the same value conditioned on the seller’s private information have the same effect on other potential acquirers, even when the security designs employed by the offers vary greatly. In fact, the incentive compatibility conditions in the competitive screening setting are the same as the conditions in the baseline setting except that the lower bound for offer acceptance when the asset is compatible is determined by rival offers rather than the seller’s reservation payoff. This change has no effect on security design.

5.5 Other extensions

5.5.1 Acquisition costs

In our baseline model, we did not include any ex ante acquirer costs associated with the effort required to conduct due diligence and develop an acquisition proposal. These costs, which are no doubt present in reality, could have been incorporated into our analysis. However, incorporating them would have complicated the analysis without appreciably affecting our conclusions. Ex ante costs are sunk at the time the acquisition is proposed to the seller, so they do not affect the offer design, which is the focus of our paper. Such costs would only ensure that acquisitions that produced sufficiently low acquirer payoffs, gross of ex ante costs, would not be undertaken.

5.5.2 Moral hazard

Implementing the value-add plan might also require ex post unobservable acquirer effort. Incorporating ex post effort into our model would add a hazard constraint to our security design model: the increase in the value of the acquirer’s expected residual share of asset cash flows produced by effort in excess of effort costs. As shown inInnes (1990), under our MLR ordering assumptions, the levered seller equity residual claim that results from issuing seller debt maximizes the acquirer’s incentive to exert effort over all claims secured only by the target’s assets. Incorporating moral hazard into our analysis would impose a constraint that might make acquisitions infeasible. However, seller debt offers would still be optimal in feasible acquisitions. Of course, all-cash offers, or offers featuring a debt claim secured by all of the acquirer’s assets, would further augment the acquirer’s effort incentives. Thus, in a setting in which the only friction is acquirer moral hazard, the nonrecourse feature of seller debt is inexplicable.

5.5.3 Seller bargaining power

The baseline model assumes that acquirers make first-and-final offers. When we reverse the allocation of bargaining power and the informed seller makes a first-and-final offer to the acquirer, the trade-offs determining the optimal security design are similar to the trade-offs in our model. A seller with compatible assets would aim for two objectives: (a) sell to an acquirer whose value-add plan relies on the compatibility, and (b) minimize the cost (given the seller’s beliefs) of satisfying the acquirer’s reservation constraint.

If the seller only considers objective (a), he would prefer to receive the most informationally sensitive claim, that is, levered seller equity. However, the seller needs to consider both objectives. With respect to objective (b), seller debt is advantageous because the divergence of the seller’s valuation and the acquirer’s valuation is largest for the upside cash flows. Thus, the cheapest way for the seller to satisfy the acquirer’s reservation constraint is to assign the upside cash flows to the acquirer, implying that the seller receives a debt claim. Consequently, the optimal security design when sellers make first-and-final offers also depends on resolving the tension between the same two objectives. For an example of the optimality of seller debt when the seller moves first, seeInternet Appendix F.

5.5.4 Underconfidence

When the acquirer lacks confidence—that is, whenFACis strongly MLR dominated byFSC—the seller values the cash flows under acquirer control more than the acquirer does. In this case, absent the seller limited liability constraint, optimal offer designs would have the acquirer propose that the seller pay a fixed salary to the acquirer at date 0 in exchange for the acquirer implementing the value-add plan. This offer would be accepted in stateCand rejected in stateI⁠. In stateC⁠, all project cash flows would be owned by the seller. The optimal offer under the seller limited liability constraint of the baseline model is an offer, by the acquirer, to manage the firm in exchange for a (risky) salary payment funded by the firm’s date 1 cash flows. Thus, in our setting, acquirers that lack confidence have no incentive to actually acquire assets from target firms. They prefer to exploit their value-add plan, which they believe is overrated by the seller, by exchanging their ability to implement the plan for a fixed salary or consulting fee.

5.5.5 Mechanism design

In the baseline model, we posited a first-and-final offer mechanism. In our baseline setting, the first-and-final mechanism is an optimal mechanism, as we show inInternet Appendix D.5. The payoff to the acquirer under the first-and-final offer mechanism equals the maximum payoff the acquirer can attain from an incentive compatible direct mechanism. Thus, the optimality of seller debt is not an artifact of the mechanism we used to implement acquirer bargaining power, that is, the first-and-final offer mechanism.
article.6 Conclusion:
Acquisitions targeting companies with a single principal owner (e.g., private firms and public firm subsidiaries) are prominent. These acquisitions are frequently motivated by the acquirer’s expectation to increase the value of the target. In such cases, it seems reasonable to presume that (1) the acquirer’s capability to add value critically depends on characteristics of the asset acquired; (2) the current asset owner has superior information about these characteristics; and (3) the acquirer is more confident than the seller about her ability to add value if the seller’s assets are compatible with the acquirer’s value-add plan.

Our analysis shows that in this scenario, even when acquirers are not liquidity-constrained or limited to certain security designs, optimal offers always take the form of cash and nonrecourse debt provided by the seller. This result is consistent with the preponderance of seller debt financing in the acquisitions of privately held firms. This phenomenon does not appear to have an alternative explanation that is grounded in direct applications of extant theoretical models to asset acquisitions.

In addition, as Hansen (1987 , p. 79) points out, “If the acquirer is too large relative to the target, there will be only a negligible contingent-pricing effect.” Using a sample of 5,229 acquisitions of private firms and public firm subsidiaries from Capital IQ, we find that the median acquiring company is 70 times the size of the target.

2 See Internet Appendix E . Also, to solve our model we assume that acquirers make acquisition offers, which is a standard assumption in the literature ( Hansen 1987 ; Fishman 1989 ). We also assume that cash flow distributions are continuous and supported by the nonnegative real line. Internet Appendix F shows that these choices are not essential for the optimality of seller debt.

3 MAC clauses are “one of the most important and carefully negotiated terms” of acquisition agreements, according to Schwartz (2009) . He argues that MACs are the analog of the frustration doctrine in common contract law, which stipulates that one party to a contract can be excused from performing when information reveals that the counterparty’s performance cannot confer the benefit that principally motivated the party to enter into the contract. The fact that a body of common-law precedents, based on judges’ decisions in actual cases, resulted in a legal doctrine centered on incompatibility suggests that incompatibility is real-world relevant. In our setting, an incompatible asset is an asset that cannot confer the intended benefit of the acquisition (i.e., the ability to implement the value-add plan). Thus, information revealing incompatibility is an exemplary case of a material adverse change.

4 Acquirer confidence is well-motivated by the behavioral finance literature, where numerous prior studies have supported the hypothesis of managerial overconfidence (e.g., Malmendier and Tate 2008 ; Hirshleifer, Low, and Teoh 2012 ; Camerer and Lovallo 1999 ). Our assumption that confidence is only exhibited if the asset is compatible is consistent with the psychology literature that argues that overconfidence is domain specific (e.g., Klayman et al. 1999 ; Larkin and Leider 2012 ).

5 Issuing recourse debt is a stronger signal of buyer quality than issuing nonrecourse debt. Thus, it is easier to rationalize seller debt in an informed-seller setting like ours than in a buyer-signaling setting.

Since sellers in asset acquisition models receive claims that depend on the stand-alone value of their assets only, this channel cannot operate in asset acquisition settings.

In Simsek, “upside/downside optimism,” is a relationship between possible optimist distributions: the upside optimist’s distribution is more “skewed to the upside” than the downside optimist’s distribution (see Definition 3 in Simsek).
